text: China attracts new foreign investors who travel frequently and value fast transportation. What happens in the market for airline service between Beijing and Shanghai?
feedback: The number of buyers rises. At every price, more people want airline service, so demand shifts right.

text: The quality of a no-toll highway connecting two cities deteriorates. What happens to demand for airline service between those cities?

options.text: Demand for airline service shifts left.
Demand for airline service shifts right.
Supply of airline service shifts right.
Only price changes; no curve shifts.

feedback: The highway is a substitute for air travel. When the substitute becomes less attractive, more travelers switch toward airline service, shifting demand right.

text: The price of airplane fuel rises sharply. Which curve shifts in the market for airline service?
feedback: Fuel is an input cost. Higher input costs make production more expensive, so firms supply less at every price and the supply curve shifts left.

text: The government lowers the regulated price of airline service. What happens to the demand and supply curves?

options.text: Demand shifts right and supply shifts left.
Demand shifts left and supply shifts right.
No curve shifts; the price change causes movement along the curves.

feedback: A change in the good's own price causes movement along demand and supply. A curve shifts only when another factor changes.

text: The demand curve is \(P = 210 - 10Q_D\). A decrease in the price of a substitute reduces demand by 10 units at every price. Which new demand curve represents that leftward shift?
feedback: A 10-unit decrease in quantity demanded at every price means the curve shifts left. Keeping the same slope, the new intercept is 110, so the new curve is \(P = 110 - 10Q_D\).

text: The supply curve is \(P = 4Q_S + 10\). A technology improvement saves suppliers $2 per unit. What is the new supply curve?
feedback: The technology improvement lowers the price producers require for any quantity by $2. The slope stays the same, and the intercept falls from 10 to 8.

text: An outward shift in both demand and supply moves equilibrium quantity from 4 to 7.5. What is the change in equilibrium quantity?
feedback: The change is \(7.5 - 4 = 3.5\). When both curves shift outward, equilibrium quantity rises.

text: If both demand and supply shift outward, what can we conclude about the new equilibrium price and quantity?

options.text: Quantity increases; price may rise, fall, or stay the same.

feedback: Both outward shifts increase equilibrium quantity. Price depends on which curve shifts more: demand pressure pushes price up, while supply pressure pushes price down.
